resources.string:
Recusrively scanning in:
- WheelLog/
- EUC World/

from: %1$s

Ensure your logs are in those folders.
PDF saved — tap SHARE to send it
CSV saved — tap SHARE to send it
Archive saved — tap SHARE to send it
These parameters are used to segregate R_batt from R_mosfet during analysis.
• Coeff temp : +1%%/°C is usual for MOSFETs
Name from logs — cannot be overridden
Cells in parallel per group (1P, 2P, 3P…) — default 1
EUC SoH focuses on computing statistics from local euc logs parsing. Everything happens and stays on your device.
What this tool does — and does not do

EUC SoH reads the CSV logs produced by WheelLog or EUC World and tracks how your battery pack behaves over thousands of kilometres. Its main question: is the pack getting harder to push current through? Like an old AA battery that still reads 1.5 V but cannot light a bright LED anymore, an ageing EUC pack loses its ability to deliver current cleanly — voltage drops more under load, heat rises. EUC SoH measures that drift ride after ride and warns you when it crosses meaningful thresholds.
It does not predict an exact remaining lifespan.
It does not replace hands-on testing (oscilloscope, controlled charge/discharge cycle, or a BMS — Battery Management System — diagnostic tool).
It does not compare your wheel to other riders — each wheel is its own reference.
Indicators are relative, not absolute: a high R_eq (internal resistance of the circuit) does not mean the battery is bad in isolation — only that it has drifted from its own past baseline.
How to act on the results
Alarms compare each new ride to the history of that same wheel. The app computes an average and a typical spread from all past rides, and flags how far the latest values sit from that baseline:

The value is far outside the normal range for this wheel. Reduce aggressive riding (hard acceleration, riding with a low battery), check your connectors, and consider a professional inspection before any long trip.
The value is drifting away from its baseline. Nothing urgent, but worth keeping an eye on. Check more regularly.
Metrics within the expected range for this wheel\'s history.

When a danger alarm fires on R_eq or R_batt: avoid riding with a nearly empty battery, avoid sustained hard riding (steep hills, heavy load). Use the trend and CUSUM charts (a statistical algorithm that spots when a slow drift becomes significant) to find when the degradation started.
How the app knows what \"normal\" resistance looks like

To know whether your measured resistance is alarming, the app needs a reference: what would a healthy new pack of the same type measure? It estimates this automatically from the maximum voltage seen in your logs — no need to enter the cell model manually. A higher pack voltage means more cells in series, which means higher total resistance.\n\nFrom that maximum voltage, the app deduces the number of cells in series and looks up a typical internal resistance for new cells of that chemistry (18650 or 21700 format) from public datasheets.
R_pack_nominal = S × r_cell / P\n\nwhere S = series cell count, P = parallel cell count (configurable, default 1)

This reference value acts as an absolute safety net: even if your wheel has always measured high (so the statistical baseline is already shifted), an alarm fires if R_eq exceeds a configurable multiple of the expected new-pack value (default 1.8×). This catches wheels that were already degraded when you first started logging.

A 100V wheel typically has 24S cells. With r_cell = 80 mΩ (fresh 21700) and P = 1: R_nominal = 24 × 0.080 / 1 = 1.92 Ω. A measured R_eq_median of 3.5 Ω would be ~1.8× nominal, triggering the absolute alarm.
The P value can be set in the MOSFET config dialog (field \"N parallel cells\"). It does not affect resistance computation — only the R_nominal alarm threshold.
Key metrics and how to read them

The main health number. Measures how hard the whole power circuit resists current flow during a ride — like friction in a pipe. Higher = more resistance = more voltage lost under load = less safety margin.\n\nComputed as the median across all high-speed, high-load samples in a ride.

R_eq_median is the primary alarm trigger. It captures degradation regardless of origin — cells, power transistors (MOSFETs), or wiring — but does not tell you what is failing.\n\nTo attribute the drift: rising SAG and R_batt point to the cells; rising temp_board_max with stable SAG points to the MOSFETs. The other metrics explain what R_eq_median summarises.

The resistance of the cells only — with the contribution of the power transistors (MOSFETs) and wiring subtracted out. Temperature-corrected to 25°C so winter and summer rides are comparable.\n\nOnly available if you have entered MOSFET parameters in the settings. Without them, R_eq_median_25C is shown instead — it includes the full circuit.

Slow increase over hundreds of rides = normal cell ageing. Sudden jump = possible cell failure, bad connector, or BMS (Battery Management System) fault. Flat while R_eq rises = cells are fine, look at the MOSFETs or wiring.

Estimated resistance of the power transistors (MOSFETs) at their operating temperature. Computed from the datasheet on-resistance you entered, scaled by how much resistance increases with heat. This is a fixed model — it does not update as the transistors age.

Because R_ds(on)_25°C is a fixed constant in the model, R_mosfet_hot does not directly track MOSFET ageing. What it captures is the thermal variation around that fixed baseline.

Real MOSFET degradation (increased on-state resistance due to oxide wear, bond fatigue, etc.) will appear in R_eq_median rising over time — not in R_mosfet_hot alone.

The diagnostic signature of MOSFET failure is: R_eq_median trending up + temp_board_max increasing at comparable power conditions + SAG remaining relatively stable (cells still healthy). If SAG and R_batt also rise, the degradation is more widespread.

How much the voltage drops under hard load. Like squeezing a stiff water bottle: an ageing pack has to work harder to push the same current, and voltage collapses more. Measured as the gap between resting voltage and actual voltage during high-demand moments.

High sag shrinks the gap between actual voltage and the firmware\'s cut-off threshold. Especially dangerous at low battery or in cold weather — the firmware may cut power unexpectedly during hard acceleration.
The lowest voltage the pack reached during a hard-load moment. The closer this gets to your firmware\'s cut-off voltage, the less margin you have before a sudden power cut.
Decreasing over time = safety margin shrinking. Value dropping below your firmware\'s cut-off threshold means a sudden power cut is possible under hard acceleration.

The hottest the controller board got during a ride. Used as a proxy for the temperature inside the power transistors, which run hotter than the board surface but cannot be measured directly from logs.

An upward trend at similar riding intensity means heat builds up faster than before — a sign of degraded thermal conductivity (dried-out thermal paste, ageing transistors). High sustained temperatures accelerate failure.
How hard the pack is being pushed electrically. High current = high stress on both the cells and the power transistors.
Alarms on these metrics indicate unusually aggressive riding sessions (high acceleration, steep terrain). High currents accelerate both battery and MOSFET degradation.
EUC SoH is open-source research software. Results are indicative only. Always prioritize safety and consult a qualified technician for maintenance decisions.

A measure of the total heat load put through the power stage per ride. Doubling the current quadruples the heat (current² effect) — so a short aggressive session can carry more thermal weight than a long calm one. Normalized per kilometre for fair comparison between rides.

Each point is one ride. A spike means that session was electrically intense. The dashed line (right axis) accumulates the total thermal dose from ride 1 to today — use it to assess the overall stress history of your wheel.\n\nOnly available when your logging app records phase current (EUC World or WheelLog with that column enabled).
R_eq_median fluctuates around a flat baseline across thousands of kilometres. Points stay inside the green–orange band with no upward trend. This is what a healthy, stable pack looks like.

R_eq_median climbs steadily from ~0.12 Ω to over 0.40 Ω across 27 000 km, crossing the warning band and eventually the Danger threshold. The last sessions spike sharply. This is accelerating degradation — the rate of climb matters as much as the absolute value.
Red dashed line: Danger threshold (µ + 2σ or absolute)

DarknessBot does not distinguish phase current from battery current. If your wheel exposes phase current (Gotway/Begode in particular), R_eq and R_batt calculations will be physically incorrect.\n\nEnable for exploration only — do not use to diagnose real battery state of health.

Ohm's law: voltage drop = current × resistance. When the wheel draws current, the pack voltage dips below what it would be at rest. The bigger the dip for the same current, the higher the resistance.

For each ride, the app picks samples at speed > 20 km/h with a meaningful current draw:

R_eq = (V_idle − V_measured) / |I_battery|

V_idle is the local resting voltage (reconstructed from brief low-current moments during the ride). R_eq_median is the median of all per-sample values across the ride.

The resting voltage of a battery changes as it discharges (State of Charge effect). Using a single voltage from the start of a ride would introduce errors over long sessions.\n\nThe app finds brief moments where current is near zero (|I| < 3 A for at least 5 s, no abrupt voltage swing) and interpolates a smooth curve through them. This gives a local resting voltage for every moment of the ride, tracking SoC naturally without an explicit SoC measurement.
R_mosfet and R_batt — splitting the resistance

R_eq_median is always computed. It is then normalized to 25°C via Arrhenius to give reqMedian25C — this is the temperature-corrected equivalent resistance of the full circuit (cells + MOSFETs + wiring).

R_batt (the battery resistance isolated from the rest) is only computed when MOSFET parameters are configured:
R_mosfet(T) = R_ds(on)_25°C × (1 + α × (T − 25)) + R_wiring
R_batt = max(0, R_eq_median − R_mosfet(T_board))
R_batt_median_25C = Arrhenius correction of R_batt

Without MOSFET config, R_batt and R_batt_median_25C are not shown. The code does not assume R_eq ≈ R_batt — it simply does not attempt the split.

A cold battery measures higher internal resistance than a warm one — even in perfect health. To make rides comparable across seasons, R_batt is corrected to a reference temperature of 25°C.

The correction uses the Arrhenius equation (a standard chemistry model for how rates — and resistance — change with temperature):

R_25°C = R_measured / exp((Ea/R_gas) × (1/T_meas − 1/298.15))

Ea (activation energy) defaults to 20 kJ/mol, typical for Li-ion. If your logs span at least 5°C of temperature variation, the app can calibrate Ea automatically from your own riding history.

This metric integrates the square of the phase current over the ride duration:

I²_int = (∫ I_phase² dt) / T_ride

The integral is computed with the trapezoid rule using actual timestamps when available, or a 0.1 s fallback. Squaring the current makes it proportional to Joule heating — doubling the current quadruples the heat. Normalizing by ride duration makes a long calm ride and a short aggressive ride comparable on a per-ride basis.

The chart displays both the per-ride dose (left axis) and a cumulative sum from ride 1 to N (right axis, dashed grey line).

I_phase2_Int is only available when your logger records phase current (EUC World or WheelLog with that column enabled).

R_pack_nominal = S × r_cell / P

S = number of cells in series (derived from max pack voltage)
P = number of parallel cell strings (configurable, default 1)
r_cell = typical DC internal resistance for a fresh cell of the detected chemistry

The P value can be set in the MOSFET config dialog. It does not affect resistance computation — only the R_nominal alarm threshold multiplier.

R_mosfet_hot = R_ds(on)_25°C × (1 + α × (T_board − 25)) + R_wiring

R_ds(on)_25°C = on-state resistance from the MOSFET datasheet (entered in settings)
α = linear thermal coefficient (default 0.007 /°C for Si MOSFETs)
R_wiring = wiring resistance estimate (configurable)

Because R_ds(on)_25°C is a fixed constant, R_mosfet_hot does not track transistor ageing. Real degradation appears as R_eq_median rising while R_mosfet_hot stays flat.

R_eq_median is always computed. It is normalised to 25°C via Arrhenius to give R_eq_median_25C.

When MOSFET parameters are configured:
 R_mosfet(T) = R_ds(on)_25°C × (1 + α × (T − 25)) + R_wiring
 R_batt = max(0, R_eq_median − R_mosfet(T_board))
 R_batt_median_25C = Arrhenius correction of R_batt

Without MOSFET config, R_batt and R_batt_median_25C are not computed — the code does not assume R_eq ≈ R_batt.

I²_int = (∫ I_phase² dt) / T_ride

The integral is computed with the trapezoid rule using actual timestamps when available, or a 0.1 s fallback. Squaring the current makes the metric proportional to Joule heating (P = R·I²). Normalising by ride duration makes long calm rides and short aggressive ones comparable on a per-ride basis.

The chart shows both the per-ride dose (left axis) and a cumulative sum from ride 1 to N (right axis, dashed grey line).
